tasks.title: Buy CT Pros domain + wire real website/email

prep.understand: The app currently has blank or placeholder values for COMPANY_WEBSITE and COMPANY_EMAIL that appear on every invoice and proposal PDF. CT Pros LLC is registered in CT — the domain should match the brand. Once bought, you'll need a real email address and to update the app settings.

Set up a business email (e.g. info@ctpros.com) via Zoho Mail or Google Workspace — free tier is fine for now.
Update COMPANY_WEBSITE and COMPANY_EMAIL in the app's Settings or `.env` with the real values.
Generate a test invoice PDF and verify the domain/email appear correctly before calling it done.

tasks.title: Durable job queue (lease/heartbeat/reclaim, window-aware, priority, cap)
tasks.notes: Core job queue for the sweeper with lease/heartbeat/reclaim, work-window awareness, priority ordering, and a concurrency cap.

prep.what: Build the Postgres-backed job queue that the AI sweeper runs on — with lease-based claiming, heartbeat keep-alive, stale-lease reaper, work-window awareness, priority ordering, and a per-repo concurrency cap.
prep.understand: This is the foundation for Phase 6. Without a durable queue, the sweeper can't run safely across VPS restarts or concurrent workers. Key pattern: a job has a `lease_expires_at`; a worker claims it atomically (`UPDATE WHERE status='queued' ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`), sends heartbeats to extend the lease, and a background reaper resets expired leases back to queued. Window-awareness: jobs that can't run outside the 5h work window check current time before claiming. Priority + cap ensures high-priority agent fixes don't get blocked by a pile of low-priority linting jobs.
approach: Add a `sweeper_jobs` table migration: id, task_id (FK), status (queued/running/done/failed), priority (int), window_eligible (bool), worker_id, lease_expires_at, heartbeat_at, attempts, max_attempts, created_at, updated_at.

Implement `claimNextJob(workerId, windowNow)`: atomic `UPDATE ... WHERE status='queued' AND (window_eligible=false OR windowNow) ORDER BY priority DESC LIMIT 1` — returns the claimed job or null (no work / outside window).
Implement `heartbeat(jobId, workerId)`: extends `lease_expires_at` by config interval; rejected if worker_id doesn't match (safety).
Implement `reclaimStaleLease()`: scheduled every 30s — resets rows where `status='running' AND lease_expires_at < now() AND attempts < max_attempts` back to `queued`; sets `failed` on exhausted ones.
Write unit tests: claim returns highest-priority job; heartbeat extends lease; stale lease reclaimed; window gate blocks outside hours.
resources: VPS Postgres `todos_platform` DB (existing Phase 3 connection via `lib/pg-board-repository.ts`)
`db/migrate.ts` migration runner for the new table
Phase 5 triage queue as a reference for the existing job patterns
Postgres `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` docs for the atomic claim pattern
Work-window config: 09:00-18:00

tasks.title: Daemon-side cross-process refresh lock (prereq for live SWEEPER_REPOS)
tasks.notes: The refresh CLI uses a per-repo O_EXCL lockfile. The daemon must honor the SAME lock. Until done: run refresh ONLY while the sweeper is stopped.

prep.what: Make the sweeper daemon acquire the same per-repo file lock the refresh CLI uses, so the two processes can't race and corrupt a repo mid-refresh.
prep.understand: Right now the CLI refresh takes a per-repo `O_EXCL` lockfile at `SWEEPER_STATE_DIR/repos/<slug>.lock`. The daemon uses an in-process lock — invisible to the CLI. If both run at the same time, the sweeper starts a job on code that the CLI is currently pulling/rebuilding. The fix: before the daemon starts a job for repo X, it must try to acquire the SAME file-based lock. If it can't (CLI is refreshing), it defers the job — not fails, just waits. Release it when the job lifecycle for that repo ends.
approach: Find the lock file path in the CLI refresh code (likely `path.join(SWEEPER_STATE_DIR, 'repos', slug + '.lock')`).

In the daemon's job-start path, before spawning a worktree or any per-repo work: `fs.openSync(lockPath, fs.constants.O_CREAT | fs.constants.O_EXCL | fs.constants.O_WRONLY)` — if EEXIST, defer the job (re-queue or skip this cycle).
In the daemon's job-complete/fail cleanup: `fs.unlinkSync(lockPath)` to release.
Add an integration test: open the lockfile, attempt a daemon job claim for that repo, assert it defers correctly.
Update the notes on t29 — the 'run refresh only while sweeper is stopped' caveat can be removed once this lands.
resources: CLI refresh lock implementation (look for `O_EXCL` or `.lock` in the sweeper source)
`SWEEPER_STATE_DIR` env var / config
Node.js `fs.constants.O_EXCL` docs for cross-process exclusive lock
The daemon's job lifecycle code (find where it starts per-repo work)

tasks.title: Unprivileged CI lane + sensitive-path human gate
tasks.notes: No secrets, no pull_request_target. Sensitive-path changes (auth, payments, CI config) need human approval even if the quality gate passes.

prep.what: Build the CI lane that runs the quality gate with zero-secret access, plus a hard gate that blocks auto-merge for any change touching sensitive paths.
prep.understand: The sweeper auto-ships code. Two security invariants must hold: (1) the CI runner has no secrets — no production tokens, no deployment creds, and especially no `pull_request_target` (GitHub's supply-chain exploit vector). (2) Even if tests pass, certain paths MUST have a human in the loop before merge — anything touching auth, payments, database migrations, CI config, or `.env` files. The Phase 5 decision inbox already handles human approval flows; you're wiring the trigger into the CI gate.
approach: Define the CI job: runs `npm test`, `npm run lint`, `npm run build` with a read-only `GITHUB_TOKEN` and NO additional secrets injected.
Implement `isSensitivePath(filePaths: string[]): boolean` — return true if any path matches: `**/*auth*`, `**/stripe*`, `**/migrations/**`, `**/.env*`, `**/ci/**`, `**/deploy/**`, `**/security*`.
After quality gate passes: if `isSensitivePath(changedFiles)` → set job status to `needs_human_review` and push a decision card to the Phase 5 inbox.
Only the inbox `approve` action triggers the actual merge/deploy step — the sweeper is never trusted to bypass this.
Write tests: a change to `auth/login.ts` must pause for human gate; a change to `lib/utils.ts` must auto-proceed after green CI.
resources: Phase 5 decision inbox code (createDecisionCard, answer→fire-agent flow from t19)
GitHub Actions workflow syntax + `GITHUB_TOKEN` permissions docs
Sensitive path glob list — expand as you audit the real repo structure
Existing sweeper quality gate code (t21 stub)
GitHub docs on `pull_request_target` attack vector
